subsystem: systemd-logind input-button handling. The function button_open(Button *b) opens a Linux evdev input device node under /dev/input/, queries the kernel for its capabilities and human-readable name via ioctl, and wires the file descriptor into the sd-event main loop so that key/switch events (power/suspend/lid buttons) are dispatched. It is the per-device setup path that turns a discovered input node into a monitored event source.
provenance: Identified from unmistakable systemd idioms and API names: strjoina() (systemd's alloca-based string join), safe_close(), log_warning_errno()/log_debug_errno()/log_error_errno(), SYNTHETIC_ERRNO(), sd_event_source_unref(), sd_event_add_io(), and the Button type with fields fd/name/manager/io_event_source. The /dev/input/ path plus the evdev ioctls EVIOCGNAME and the referenced button_suitable()/button_set_mask()/button_dispatch() helpers place this squarely in src/login/logind-button.c. The header comment '/* sample: primevul_c_train_3170_fixed */' marks it as a curated corpus sample; that banner is metadata, not part of the reviewed logic.
trust_boundary: The trust boundary is the kernel evdev interface for a physical input device. A physically present attacker who plugs in a malicious USB HID device fully controls the strings that device advertises: the EVIOCGNAME device-name string returned at line 24 is derived from attacker-supplied USB descriptor strings and can be made arbitrarily long and non-NUL-terminated. The device node selector b->name (used to build the /dev/input/ path at line 10) originates upstream from udev enumeration and is trusted-by-convention but is not validated here. Everything the ioctl writes into the local buffer 'name' must therefore be treated as hostile, unbounded, and possibly unterminated content.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares button_open, which takes a pointer to a Button object and returns an int status (0 on success, negative errno-style on failure). The locals are: 'p', a char pointer that will point at a path string; 'name', a fixed 256-byte stack buffer that will receive the device's human-readable name from the kernel; and 'r', a scratch int for return codes. 'name' is a fixed-size destination buffer with no separate length variable, so its safe use depends entirely on whatever fills it respecting the 256-byte bound and NUL-terminating the contents.

A runtime assertion that the Button pointer is non-NULL. This documents the precondition that the caller must pass a valid object. Because every subsequent statement dereferences b (b->fd, b->name, b->manager, b->io_event_source), the whole function relies on b being valid; assert() is the only guard and it is compiled out when NDEBUG is defined.

Closes any file descriptor previously stored on the button and resets the slot. safe_close() is the systemd idiom that tolerates a negative/invalid fd and always returns -1, so after this line b->fd is guaranteed to be -1. This makes the reopen path idempotent and prevents leaking a stale descriptor if button_open is called twice on the same object.

Builds the device node path by concatenating the fixed prefix '/dev/input/' with the button's device name b->name. strjoina() allocates the result on the current stack frame via alloca, so 'p' is valid only for the remainder of this function. The final path is entirely determined by b->name, which is not sanitized here.

Opens the device read/write with sensible flags: O_CLOEXEC to avoid fd inheritance across exec, O_NOCTTY so the device cannot become a controlling terminal, and O_NONBLOCK for event-driven reads. The result is stored directly into b->fd (so ownership lives on the Button, not in a local). On failure it logs a warning and returns the negative errno; note there is nothing to clean up here because b->fd is already -1 from line 8.

Calls button_suitable() to decide whether this evdev device exposes keys/switches logind cares about. A negative return is a hard error (logged and returned). A zero return means the device is uninteresting; the function logs at debug level with a synthetic EADDRNOTAVAIL errno and returns. On this r==0 early return the descriptor opened at line 12 is left stored in b->fd and is not closed here — it remains owned by the Button object for later teardown by the object's destructor.

Issues the evdev EVIOCGNAME ioctl to fetch the device's human-readable name into the 256-byte 'name' buffer. EVIOCGNAME(sizeof(name)) encodes the buffer capacity (256) into the ioctl request number so the kernel knows how many bytes it may write. On error the code logs and jumps to the fail label. Crucially, the ioctl's non-negative return value (the number of bytes actually copied) is discarded — only the <0 error case is examined — so the code does not learn whether the name was truncated.

Configures the evdev event mask on the device (which event types logind wants delivered). The (void) cast explicitly discards the return value, treating mask configuration as best-effort — a failure here is intentionally non-fatal and processing continues.

First releases any previously-registered IO event source (unref returns NULL, clearing the slot). Then registers b->fd with the manager's sd-event loop for EPOLLIN readability, so that button_dispatch(b) is invoked when input events arrive; the new source handle is stored in b->io_event_source. On registration failure it logs and goes to fail. This is where b->manager and b->manager->event are dereferenced, so both must be valid.

what_the_code_does.code: log_info("Watching system buttons on /dev/input/%s (%s)", b->name, name);
what_the_code_does.explanation: Emits an informational log line reporting the device node (b->name) and its human-readable name (the 'name' buffer filled by EVIOCGNAME). The '%s' conversion for 'name' treats the buffer as a NUL-terminated C string and reads it byte-by-byte until a NUL is found. This is the consumption point for the attacker-influenced name content.

On success returns 0. The fail label is reached from the EVIOCGNAME error path and the sd_event_add_io error path; it closes b->fd (resetting it to -1 via safe_close) and returns the saved error code r. This cleanup path correctly releases the descriptor opened at line 12 for the two failure branches that reach it.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The evdev EVIOCGNAME ioctl does NOT guarantee NUL termination. The kernel implementation (str_to_user in drivers/input/evdev.c) copies min(strlen(name)+1, size) bytes; when the real device name is 256 bytes or longer, exactly 256 bytes are written into 'name' with NO trailing NUL. The code only checks the return for <0 and discards the byte count, so it never terminates the buffer or bounds it by the returned length. A physically present attacker with a malicious USB device can advertise a name string >= 256 bytes to force this truncated, unterminated state. The invariant the implementation must uphold is: after EVIOCGNAME succeeds, force termination, e.g. name[MIN((size_t)r, sizeof(name)-1)] = 0 (or at minimum name[sizeof(name)-1] = 0) BEFORE using 'name' as a string. Because that invariant is not enforced here, the buffer flows unterminated into the %s at line 38 — see the next step.

what_could_go_wrong.code: log_info("Watching system buttons on /dev/input/%s (%s)", b->name, name);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the sink for the possibly-unterminated 'name' buffer. The '%s' conversion scans forward from &name[0] until it finds a NUL byte; if EVIOCGNAME filled all 256 bytes without terminating (previous step), the format engine reads past the end of the 256-byte stack buffer into adjacent stack memory (return address region, saved registers, 'p', 'r', or padding) until it happens upon a zero byte. Impact: an out-of-bounds stack read that discloses adjacent stack contents into the system log (information leak), or a crash if the scan runs into an unmapped page. This is attacker-triggerable by plugging in a device with a >=256-byte name. CWE-125 (out-of-bounds read), rooted in CWE-170 improper NUL termination.

assert() is the sole NULL guard on b, and it is compiled out entirely when the translation unit is built with NDEBUG. In that configuration a NULL b flows straight into b->fd at line 8 and every later member access, producing a NULL-pointer dereference and crash. This is a low-severity, defense-in-depth concern here because b is produced by internal logind enumeration rather than by an external attacker, but the contract (b must be non-NULL) is enforced only in debug builds. CWE-476.

The open() target is '/dev/input/' concatenated with b->name and nothing constrains b->name here. If b->name could ever carry path metacharacters (for example a leading '/', or '../' sequences), the resulting path would escape the /dev/input/ directory and open() would target an attacker-chosen node with O_RDWR. In practice b->name is a udev sysname (e.g. 'event3') and is trusted by convention, so this is a latent/low concern contingent on the provenance of b->name; the safe invariant is that b->name is a validated single path component with no '/' or '..'. CWE-22/CWE-20 (latent).

what_could_go_wrong.code: r = sd_event_add_io(b->manager->event, &b->io_event_source, b->fd, EPOLLIN, button_dispatch, b);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This line dereferences b->manager (to read b->manager->event) with no NULL check. If a Button can ever reach button_open with b->manager unset, this is a NULL-pointer dereference and crash. It is a latent, internal-invariant issue (logind normally links a button to its manager before opening it), not attacker-reachable across the evdev trust boundary, so severity is low. CWE-476 (latent).

On the 'device not relevant' path the function returns while b->fd still holds the descriptor opened at line 12 (it does not go through the fail label). This is intentional and correct in this form: ownership of the fd lives on the Button object, so the descriptor is released later by the object's destructor via safe_close rather than being leaked. It is called out here only to confirm it is NOT a descriptor leak (contrast with the classic bug where the fd is a bare local): the resource remains tracked. The residual, minor concern is that logind holds an open fd to an irrelevant device for the button's lifetime — a resource-retention nuance, not a security defect.

summary: This is systemd-logind's button_open() evdev setup path. The one genuine security issue is that the EVIOCGNAME ioctl at line 24 does not guarantee NUL termination of the 256-byte 'name' buffer; the code discards the returned length and never terminates the buffer, then passes it to a '%s' log at line 38, so a malicious USB device advertising a >=256-byte name causes an out-of-bounds stack read (info leak / possible crash). File-descriptor ownership and the two fail-path cleanups are handled correctly; remaining concerns (assert-only NULL guard, unvalidated b->name path component, unchecked b->manager) are low-severity/latent.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: char name[256] (fixed 256-byte buffer, no paired length variable) — line 3
reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed-size destination for EVIOCGNAME; used as a C string at line 38 without guaranteed NUL termination.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: char *p (stack pointer to strjoina/alloca result) — line 3
reviewed_anchors.reason: Points at an in-frame alloca buffer valid for the function body; no bound is copied into it here.
Plain status int, checked on each use; carries no buffer/size contract.
Only NULL guard on b; compiled out under NDEBUG, leaving later dereferences unguarded (low).
Idempotent close that always yields -1; prevents stale-fd reuse, correct.
open() target derived from unvalidated b->name; latent path-component injection if b->name is ever attacker-influenced (low).
Flag set is correct (O_CLOEXEC/O_NOCTTY/O_NONBLOCK); result stored in owned b->fd and error-checked.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: button_suitable(b) return and r<0 / r==0 branches — lines 16-22
reviewed_anchors.reason: Return code fully checked; both error and not-relevant branches handled. Fd retention on r==0 is tracked, not leaked (noted in walkthrough).
EVIOCGNAME does not guarantee NUL termination; returned byte count is discarded, so 'name' can be left unterminated.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: log_error_errno + goto fail (EVIOCGNAME error path) — lines 25-26
reviewed_anchors.reason: Error handled and routed to correct cleanup at fail label.
Best-effort mask setup with return intentionally discarded; non-fatal by design.
Releases any prior source and clears the slot; correct idempotent cleanup.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: sd_event_add_io(b->manager->event, &b->io_event_source, b->fd, EPOLLIN, button_dispatch, b) — line 32
reviewed_anchors.reason: Dereferences b->manager with no NULL check; latent NULL-deref on an internal invariant (low).
Consumes the possibly-unterminated 'name' buffer via %s — the OOB-read sink for the EVIOCGNAME contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: fail: b->fd = safe_close(b->fd); return r — lines 42-44
reviewed_anchors.reason: Correctly closes the descriptor and propagates the error for both fail-path callers.

findings.title: EVIOCGNAME result used as string without guaranteed NUL termination (OOB stack read)
findings.anchor: ioctl(b->fd, EVIOCGNAME(sizeof(name)), name) feeding log_info(..."%s"..., name)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via missing NUL termination of a kernel-filled fixed buffer
findings.invariant: After a successful EVIOCGNAME the buffer must be forcibly NUL-terminated before any string use — e.g. name[MIN((size_t)r, sizeof(name)-1)] = 0, or at minimum name[sizeof(name)-1] = 0 — because the kernel copies at most 'size' bytes and omits the terminator when the true name length >= size.
findings.failure_mode: A malicious/crafted USB HID device advertises a device-name string of 256 bytes or more. EVIOCGNAME fills all 256 bytes of 'name' with no trailing NUL; the code checks only the <0 error case and discards the returned length. At line 38 the '%s' conversion scans past the end of the 256-byte stack buffer into adjacent stack memory until it hits a zero byte, disclosing stack contents into the system log (information leak) or crashing if it reaches an unmapped page.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether any statement between line 24 and line 38 NUL-terminates 'name' (or bounds it by the ioctl return). If none does, this is a live OOB read; verify the fix terminates using the returned length.

findings.title: assert(b) is the only NULL guard; NULL-deref under NDEBUG
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0017_cwe401.c (button_open), line 6, affecting all later b-> dereferences
findings.invariant: b must be non-NULL on entry; if NULL is ever possible in release builds, an explicit if (!b) return -EINVAL check is required because assert() is removed when NDEBUG is defined.
findings.failure_mode: In an NDEBUG build a NULL Button pointer passes silently and is dereferenced at line 8 (b->fd) and beyond, crashing the process. Not attacker-reachable across the evdev boundary (b comes from internal enumeration), hence low severity.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm callers never pass NULL and whether logind is built with assertions enabled; otherwise add an explicit release-build NULL check.

findings.title: Unvalidated b->name forms the open() path component
findings.bug_class: Path component injection / improper input validation (latent)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — origin/validation of b->name is outside the shown code
findings.invariant: b->name must be a single validated path component containing no '/' and no '..' so the opened node cannot escape /dev/input/.
findings.failure_mode: If b->name could carry a leading '/' or '../' sequences, the concatenated path escapes /dev/input/ and open() with O_RDWR targets an attacker-chosen node. In practice b->name is a udev sysname and trusted by convention, so this is latent and contingent on that provenance.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace where b->name is set and confirm it is a validated device sysname with no path separators before it reaches this concatenation.

findings.title: Unchecked b->manager dereference when registering the event source
findings.code: r = sd_event_add_io(b->manager->event, &b->io_event_source, b->fd, EPOLLIN, button_dispatch, b);
findings.bug_class: NULL pointer dereference on internal invariant (latent)
findings.invariant: b->manager (and b->manager->event) must be non-NULL before button_open is called.
findings.failure_mode: If a Button reaches button_open with b->manager unset, reading b->manager->event dereferences NULL and crashes. This depends on an internal construction invariant rather than attacker input, so severity is low.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm every code path that constructs a Button links it to a valid manager before button_open runs.

audit_checklist: Verify 'name' is NUL-terminated after EVIOCGNAME before line 38 — check for name[sizeof(name)-1]=0 or termination via the ioctl return value; treat absence as a live OOB read.
Confirm whether the ioctl return (bytes copied) is used to bound the string; it is currently discarded.
Check the provenance and validation of b->name (must be a single path component, no '/'/'..') before it is joined into the /dev/input/ path.
Confirm callers guarantee non-NULL b and non-NULL b->manager, since assert() is removed under NDEBUG and b->manager is dereferenced unguarded.
Verify the r==0 (device-not-relevant) return does not leak b->fd — confirm the descriptor is released by the Button destructor since it is not closed on that path.
Confirm both fail-path branches (EVIOCGNAME error, sd_event_add_io error) close b->fd exactly once via safe_close and propagate the correct errno.
